abstract: This conference paper presents CooRnet, an R package and methodological framework for detecting Coordinated Link Sharing Behavior (CLSB) on Facebook, operationalizing Camille François's Actors-Behaviors-Content (A-B-C) disinformation framework as an iterative cycle. The authors apply CooRnet to a case study of problematic COVID-19 information circulating in Italy during the first and second pandemic waves, using fact-checked false claims as a seed to identify networks of coordinated actors. Across two iterations, the approach surfaced 152 then 344 coordinated entities, with content prominently featuring COVID-19 skepticism, anti-vaccine, and anti-mask narratives.
key_claims: Content-based and actor-list-based approaches to studying disinformation are each insufficient because debunking is slow and lists of problematic sources fail to capture the evolving nature of information operations.
An integrated approach combining Actors, Behaviors, and Content (the A-B-C framework) is needed to recognize manipulative online activity.
Coordinated Link Sharing Behavior (CLSB) is a strategy used to game platform algorithms and is consistently associated with the spread of problematic information.

CooRnet operationalizes A-B-C as a cycle that begins with harmful content, identifies deceptive coordinated behavior, surfaces manipulative actors, and then re-iterates using content from those actors.
Coordinated networks were active in amplifying problematic COVID-19 content in Italy, particularly skepticism, anti-vaccine, and anti-mask narratives.
methods: Use of the CoronaVirus Alliance / IFCN database to identify 212 Italian false or misleading claims (Jan 22 – Aug 26, 2020).
CrowdTangle Search to expand seed claims into a dataset of 1,258 related URLs.
Collection of Facebook shares of these URLs via CooRnet::get_ctshares() (N=13,271 in iteration 1; 364,400 in iteration 2).

Detection of coordinated shares via CooRnet::get_coord_shares(), defined as multiple actors sharing the same link within an unusually short time window, with a threshold on repeated co-shares to limit false positives.
Use of CooRnet::get_outputs() to produce coordinated-entity lists, marked share data, projected network graphs, component summaries, and top URLs/posts.
Iterative A-B-C cycle: feeding the URLs shared by identified actors (October 2020, 59,346 URLs) back into the pipeline for a second round of detection.
Interactive visualization of cumulative shares per URL via CooRnet::draw_url_timeline_chart().
findings: First A-B-C iteration identified 152 coordinated Facebook entities from 13,271 shares of 1,258 fact-checked-related URLs.
Second iteration, seeded with 59,346 URLs from the initial actors, produced 364,400 shares and identified 344 coordinated entities.
Top coordinately shared URLs prominently featured COVID-19 skepticism, anti-vaccine, and anti-mask content.
The iterative cycle expands the set of detected manipulative actors beyond what a single content- or list-based pass would reveal.
contributions: Introduces CooRnet, an open R package implementing CLSB detection on CrowdTangle-tracked Facebook data.
Provides a concrete operationalization of François's A-B-C disinformation framework as an iterative detection cycle.
Demonstrates the framework's applicability beyond electoral contexts by applying it to COVID-19 misinformation in Italy.
Offers a reproducible workflow and set of outputs (entity lists, component summaries, network graphs, timeline visualizations) for researchers studying coordinated inauthentic behavior.
framing: The paper positions itself within mis/disinformation studies, responding to critiques about the conceptual fuzziness of 'fake news' and the limits of content-only or actor-list-only methods. It draws on François's A-B-C framework and prior CLSB work by the authors to argue for behavior-centered, integrated detection of influence operations on social media platforms.
